Achievement of high inter-rater reliability for pedagogy-aligned evaluation scores.
Discovery that mainstream LLMs (GPT, Gemini, Llama) fail to provide strong performance out-of-the-box.
Fine-tuning Qwen3 yielded an 11% improvement in scoring and evidence accuracy on a held-out test set.
Validation of the AIME framework for automating time-consuming and expertise-intensive instructional reviews.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: SciEval researchers introduce a benchmark showing that domain-specific fine-tuning significantly improves LLM accuracy in evaluating K-12 science materials.

title: Distributed adaptive estimation for stochastic large regression models
summary: This paper studies the distributed adaptiveestimation problems for stochastic large regression modelswith an infinite number of parameters. By constructing a re-cursive local cost function, we propose a novel distributedrecursive least squares algorithm to estimate the unknownsystem parameters, where the growth rate of regressors'dimension is characterized by a non-decreasing positivefunction. The almost sure convergence of the proposedalgorithm is established under a cooperative excitationcondition, which incorporates the temporal information andthe spatial information to reflect the cooperative effectamong multiple agents. Moreover, we analyze the predic-tion error by establishing the asymptotic upper boundof the accumulated regret without any excitation condi-tions. The main difficulty of theoretical analysis lies in howto analyze properties of the product of non-independentand non-stationary random matrices, whose dimensionschange over time simultaneously. Some techniques, suchas stochastic Lyapunov function, double-array martingaletheory and algebraic graph theory, are employed to dealwith the above issue. Our theoretical results are derivedwithout imposing independence or stationarity assump-tions on the regression vectors, thereby not excluding thecorrelated feedback signals.

- Proposes a **distributed recursive least squares (DRLS)** algorithm designed for stochastic regression models where the number of parameters can grow infinitely.
- Implements a **recursive local cost function** that enables decentralized parameter estimation across multiple agents while accounting for temporal and spatial information.
- Establishes **almost sure convergence** using stochastic Lyapunov functions and algebraic graph theory without requiring independent or stationary data.
- Provides a theoretical framework for **dynamic regressor dimensions**, allowing the model complexity to scale non-decreasingly over time.

Proof of almost sure parameter convergence under cooperative excitation conditions.
Derivation of an asymptotic upper bound for the accumulated regret (prediction error).
Elimination of the requirement for independent or stationary regression vectors, allowing for correlated feedback.
Validation of the algorithm's stability using double-array martingale theory and algebraic graph techniques.

title: Benchmarking Stopping Criteria for Evolutionary Multi-objective Optimization
summary: Stopping criteria automatically determine when to stop an evolutionary algorithm, so as not to waste function evaluations on a stagnant population. Although stopping criteria play an important role in real-world applications, they have attracted little attention in the evolutionary multi-objective optimization (EMO) community. In fact, new stopping criteria for EMO have been rarely developed in recent years. One reason for the stagnation in developing stopping criteria for EMO is a lack of effective benchmarking methodologies. To address this issue, this paper proposes (i) a performance measure of stopping criteria for EMO and (ii) a file-based benchmarking approach. This paper also proposes (iii) a data representation method that effectively stores population states in text files. (i) The proposed measure represents the performance of stopping criteria as a single scalar value, making comparison easy. (ii) The proposed file-based approach not only simplifies the benchmarking process but also facilitates reproducibility. (iii) The proposed data representation method addresses the issue of file size in (ii). We demonstrate the effectiveness of our three contributions (i)--(iii) by benchmarking five representative stopping criteria for EMO.

processed.summary:
- The paper addresses the lack of standardized evaluation for **stopping criteria** in **Evolutionary Multi-objective Optimization (EMO)**, proposing a new benchmarking framework.
- It introduces a **scalar performance measure** designed to simplify the comparison of termination strategies by quantifying their efficiency and accuracy in a single value.
- The researchers implement a **file-based benchmarking approach** and a novel **data representation method** to ensure reproducibility while minimizing the storage footprint of population state logs.
key_results: Defined a single scalar metric to evaluate EMO stopping criteria performance.
Created a standardized file-based benchmarking framework for experimental consistency.
Developed a text-based data representation method to handle large population state files.
Benchmarked five representative EMO stopping criteria to demonstrate framework utility.
Established a methodology to reduce wasted function evaluations in stagnant optimization runs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a benchmarking framework and performance metrics to standardize stopping criteria evaluation for evolutionary multi-objective optimization.

title: An Investigation of Linguistic Biases in LLM-Based Recommendations
summary: We investigate linguistic biases in LLM-based restaurant and product recommendations given prompts varying across Southern American English (AE), Indian English (IE), and Code-Switched Hindi-English dialects, using the Yelp Open dataset (Yelp Inc., 2023) and Walmart product reviews dataset (PromptCloud,2020). We add lists of restaurant and product names balanced by cuisine type and product category to the prompts given to the LLM, and we zero-shot prompt the LLMs in a cold-start setting to select the top-20 restaurant and product recommendations from these lists for each of the dialect-varied prompts. We prompt LLMs using different list samples across 20 seeds for better generalization, and aggregate per cuisine-type and per category response counts for each seed, question/prompt, and LLM model. We run mixed-effects regression models for each model family and topic (restaurant/product) with the aggregate response counts as the dependent, and conduct likelihood ratio tests for the fixed effects with post-hoc pairwise testing of estimated marginal means differences, to investigate group-level differences in recommendation counts by model size and dialect type. Results show that dialect plays a role in the type of restaurant selected across the models tested with the mistral-small-3.1 model and both the llama-3.1 family models tested showing more sensitivity to Indian English and Code-Switched prompts. In terms of product recommendations, the llama-3.1-70B-model is particularly sensitive to Code-Switched prompts in four out of seven categories, and more beauty and home category recommendations are seen when using the Indian English and Code-Switched prompts for larger and smaller models, respectively. No broad trends are seen in the model-size based differences, with differing recommendations based on model sizes conditioned by the type of dialect.

processed.summary:
- The researchers evaluate **linguistic bias** in LLM-based recommendation systems by testing prompts across three distinct dialects: Southern American English, Indian English, and Code-Switched Hindi-English.
- The methodology utilizes **mixed-effects regression models** to analyze zero-shot recommendation counts from the **Llama-3.1** and **Mistral-small-3.1** families in a cold-start setting.
- The study investigates the impact of **model size and dialect type** on the distribution of restaurant cuisines and product categories selected by the models.
- The architecture of the evaluation involves **20 seeds per prompt** and balanced item lists to ensure statistical generalization across different model scales.

Llama-3.1-70B shows extreme sensitivity to Code-Switched prompts in most product categories.
Indian English prompts lead to an increase in beauty category recommendations for larger models.
Smaller models recommend more 'home' category products when prompted with Code-Switched Hindi-English.
Model size does not correlate with bias reduction; sensitivity remains dependent on specific dialect types.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that linguistic dialects in prompts significantly bias LLM recommendation outputs for restaurants and consumer products.

title: Benchmarking Logistic Regression, SVM, and LightGBM Against BiLSTM with Attention for Sentiment Analysis on Indonesian Product Reviews
summary: Sentiment analysis of product reviews on e-commerce platforms plays a critical role in automatically understanding customer satisfaction and providing actionable insights for sellers seeking to improve product quality. This paper presents a comprehensive benchmarking study comparing a Machine Learning (ML) approach via the PyCaret AutoML framework against a Deep Learning (DL) approach based on a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) architecture with an Attention mechanism for binary sentiment classification on Indonesian product reviews. The dataset comprises 19,728 samples balanced equally between positive and negative reviews. For the ML approach, three prominent algorithms were evaluated via 10-fold stratified cross-validation: Logistic Regression (LR), Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a linear kernel, and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM). Logistic Regression achieved the best ML performance with an accuracy of 97.26\% and an F1-score of 97.26\%. The BiLSTM with Attention model, evaluated on 3,946 held-out test samples, achieved an accuracy of 97.24\% and an F1-score of 97.24\%. These comparative results demonstrate that traditional ML algorithms with proper preprocessing and feature extraction can compete closely with, and even marginally outperform, more complex sequential DL architectures on high-dimensional datasets, while simultaneously offering greater computational efficiency.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **Logistic Regression**, **SVM**, and **LightGBM** against a **BiLSTM with Attention** architecture for Indonesian sentiment classification.
- Utilizes the **PyCaret AutoML framework** for the Machine Learning (ML) branch and a 10-fold stratified cross-validation strategy.
- Employs a balanced dataset of 19,728 samples to compare high-dimensional feature extraction against sequential **Deep Learning** models.
- Proves that classical ML algorithms can achieve parity with complex neural networks while offering significantly better **computational efficiency**.
key_results: Logistic Regression achieved the highest ML accuracy and F1-score at 97.26%.
BiLSTM with Attention reached a nearly identical accuracy of 97.24% on test samples.
The study confirms that traditional ML can compete with sequential DL architectures on high-dimensional text datasets.
Balanced dataset distribution (50/50 positive/negative) supported robust evaluation across all models.
SVM and LightGBM were evaluated alongside LR, with LR emerging as the superior ML classifier for this use case.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that Logistic Regression matches BiLSTM performance in Indonesian sentiment analysis while maintaining higher computational efficiency.

title: Praxy Voice: Voice-Prompt Recovery + BUPS for Commercial-Class Indic TTS from a Frozen Non-Indic Base at Zero Commercial-Training-Data Cost
summary: Commercial TTS systems produce near-native Indic audio, but the best open-source bases (Chatterbox, Indic Parler-TTS, IndicF5) trail them on measured phonological dimensions, and the most widely adopted multilingual base (Chatterbox, 23 languages) does not even tokenise Telugu or Tamil. We ask: what is the minimum intervention that brings such a non-Indic-native base to commercial-class output on Telugu, Tamil, and Hindi, without training a new acoustic decoder and without any commercial TTS training data? We combine three pieces: (1) BUPS, a Brahmic Unified Phoneme Space that deterministically romanises seven Indic scripts to ISO-15919 so Chatterbox's Latin tokeniser can process them; (2) a LoRA adapter on only the text-token predictor (Chatterbox's t3), trained on ~1,220h of licensed Indic audio with a Hindi-proxy language_id; (3) a voice-prompt recovery recipe -- an 8-11s same-language reference clip plus three sampling overrides (exaggeration 0.7, temperature 0.6, min_p 0.1; "Config B") -- that recovers commercial-class acoustic output with no acoustic-decoder training. On Hindi, the LoRA regresses accuracy and we instead use vanilla Chatterbox + Config B, giving a two-branch deployment. Evaluated on 10-utterance pilot sets with the companion PSP benchmark, Praxy Voice matches or slightly leads commercial baselines: 26.7% retroflex collapse on Telugu (vs Sarvam Bulbul 33.3%), 71% Tamil-zha collapse (vs commercial trio's 86%), 0.025 LLM-WER on Hindi (tied with Cartesia Sonic-3). For intra-sentential code-mix we add a third branch (IndicF5 + native-script transliteration) that drops code-mix LLM-WER from 0.80-0.85 to 0.14-0.27 across Hi/Te/Ta. We release R6 LoRA weights (Apache-2.0), inference code and router (MIT), and a Gradio demo.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **Praxy Voice**, an architecture that brings non-Indic-native TTS bases (like Chatterbox) to commercial-class quality for Telugu, Tamil, and Hindi without retraining acoustic decoders.
* Utilizes **BUPS (Brahmic Unified Phoneme Space)** to deterministically map Indic scripts to ISO-15919, enabling standard Latin tokenizers to process scripts they previously could not tokenize.
* Implements a **LoRA adapter** specifically on the text-token predictor and a **Voice-prompt recovery recipe** with optimized sampling parameters (exaggeration, temperature, min_p) to achieve high acoustic fidelity.
* Employs a **three-branch deployment strategy** including a specialized IndicF5 branch to handle intra-sentential code-mixing, significantly reducing Word Error Rates.
key_results: Achieved 26.7% retroflex collapse on Telugu, outperforming Sarvam Bulbul's 33.3%.
Reduced Tamil-zha collapse to 71%, significantly better than the commercial trio average of 86%.
Matched Cartesia Sonic-3 performance on Hindi with a 0.025 LLM-WER.
Dropped code-mix LLM-WER from 0.80-0.85 to 0.14-0.27 using an IndicF5-based third branch.
Released R6 LoRA weights under Apache-2.0 and inference code under MIT for open-source accessibility.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Praxy Voice researchers introduce a LoRA-based recovery method enabling non-Indic models to match commercial-grade TTS performance across three major languages.

title: Analysis of DNA thermal stability across a broad range of thionine concentrations
summary: Interest in studying the interaction of small molecules with DNA is caused by the need to develop new, highly effective, and low-toxic drugs for cancer treatment. The strong and highly specific binding of thionine with DNA makes it a promising candidate for use in medicine and pharmacology. In this study, DNA-thionine complexes in aqueous solutions were investigated using UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy. The thermal stability of native DNA was studied in a broad range of thionine concentrations. The mechanisms of thionine binding to DNA, depending on the concentration of thionine, have been established. At low thionine concentrations $([c_{th}] \le 1.5 \text{ mg/L})$, thionine molecules intercalate between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. At a thionine concentration of 1.5-10 mg/L, the groove binding and external electrostatic interaction of positively charged thionine with negatively charged biopolymer phosphate groups of the DNA backbones is preferable. In all cases, the interaction of thionine with DNA leads to an increase in the thermal stability of the polynucleotide. These findings provide valuable insight into the concentration-dependent molecular mechanisms of DNA-small molecule interactions, supporting the rational design of anticancer and antimicrobial agents, as well as exploiting molecular probes for nucleic acid detection, imaging, and other biomedical applications.

processed.summary:
- The study utilizes **UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy** to analyze the interaction between DNA and thionine molecules across varying concentrations.
- Findings reveal a transition in **molecular binding mechanisms**, moving from base pair intercalation to groove binding as concentration increases.
- The research establishes that **DNA-thionine interactions** universally enhance the thermal stability of the polynucleotide, offering insights for **pharmacological drug design**.
key_results: Thionine molecules intercalate between base pairs at concentrations under 1.5 mg/L.
Groove binding becomes the preferred mechanism at concentrations between 1.5 and 10 mg/L.
Electrostatic interactions occur between positively charged thionine and negative DNA phosphate groups.
Interaction with thionine consistently increases the thermal stability of native DNA.
The results provide a rationale for using thionine in anticancer agents and molecular probes.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate how thionine concentration influences DNA binding mechanisms and enhances thermal stability for biomedical applications.

title: Augmented reality system for visualising magnetic field topology and charged-particle trajectories in magnetic fusion plasmas
summary: A cost-effective augmented reality (AR) system is presented for visualising three-dimensional magnetic field structures and charged-particle trajectories in magnetically confined fusion plasmas. The system presented in this study integrates an orbit-following simulation code with a marker-based AR framework using a web camera and the OpenCV library. By synchronizing the time step of the simulation with the frame rate of the camera, the trajectories are continuously updated and superimposed in real time onto the camera image. Through the interactive operation of manipulating the web camera, users can observe three-dimensional structures, such as magnetic islands, from various positions and viewing angles. Simultaneously, the visualisation results can be shared by multiple people through a display. Such a shared AR environment supports an intuitive understanding of three-dimensional spatial structures that involve a high cognitive load. It also enables collaborative reasoning based on common visual information in research on magnetic confinement fusion, where researchers and students have diverse backgrounds in physics, engineering, and related fields.

processed.summary:
* Developed a **cost-effective AR framework** that integrates orbit-following simulation code with **marker-based tracking** via the OpenCV library.
* Implemented **real-time superimposition** by synchronizing simulation time steps with hardware frame rates for continuous visual updates of particle trajectories.
* Facilitates **collaborative reasoning** by allowing multiple researchers to interactively analyze complex 3D magnetic structures, such as magnetic islands, from diverse viewing angles.
key_results: Integration of numerical simulation code with real-time marker-based AR tracking.
Visualization of 3D magnetic field structures and charged-particle trajectories in real time.
Synchronization of simulation processing with camera frame rates for smooth overlays.
Support for multi-user shared environments to enable collaborative physics analysis.
Reduction of cognitive load for interpreting complex three-dimensional spatial data.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop an AR system using OpenCV to visualize plasma trajectories, facilitating collaborative scientific reasoning.

title: Do LLMs Capture Embodied Cognition and Cultural Variation? Cross-Linguistic Evidence from Demonstratives
summary: Do large language models (LLMs) truly acquire embodied cognition and cultural conventions from text? We introduce demonstratives, fundamental spatial expressions like "this/that" in English and "zhè/nà" in Chinese, as a novel probe for grounded knowledge. Using 6,400 responses from 320 native speakers, we establish a human baseline: English speakers reliably distinguish proximal-distal referents but struggle with perspective-taking, while Chinese speakers switch perspectives fluently but tolerate distal ambiguity. In contrast, five state-of-the-art LLMs fail to inherently understand the proximal-distal contrast and show no cultural differences, defaulting to English-centric reasoning. Our study contributes (i) a new task, based on demonstratives, as a new lens for evaluating embodied cognition and cultural conventions; (ii) empirical evidence of cross-cultural asymmetries in human interpretation; (iii) a new perspective on the egocentric-sociocentric debate, showing both orientations coexist but vary across languages; and (iv) a call to address individual variation in future model design.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel **evaluation benchmark** using spatial demonstratives ("this" vs "that") to measure **embodied cognition** and cultural grounding in LLMs.
- Conducts a cross-linguistic study comparing 6,400 human responses against five **state-of-the-art LLMs** to identify gaps in perspective-taking.
- Reveals that current models lack an inherent understanding of **proximal-distal contrasts** and fail to reflect cultural nuances in spatial reasoning.
- Critiques the **text-only training paradigm** for its inability to capture sociocentric orientations and grounded human experiences.
key_results: LLMs failed to reliably distinguish between proximal and distal referents compared to human baselines.
Human English speakers prioritize egocentric views, while Chinese speakers demonstrate fluid perspective-switching.
All tested LLMs defaulted to English-centric reasoning regardless of the language being processed.
Models showed zero significant cultural variation, failing to replicate human interpretation asymmetries.
The study identifies a coexistence of egocentric and sociocentric orientations in humans that is missing in AI.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that SOTA LLMs lack embodied cognition and fail to capture cultural variations in spatial reasoning.

title: FED-FSTQ: Fisher-Guided Token Quantization for Communication-Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices
summary: Federated fine-tuning provides a practical route to adapt large language models (LLMs) on edge devices without centralizing private data, yet in mobile deployments the training wall-clock is often bottlenecked by straggler-limited uplink communication under heterogeneous bandwidth and intermittent participation. Although parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) reduces trainable parameters, per-round payloads remain prohibitive in non-IID regimes, where uniform compression can discard rare but task-critical signals. We propose Fed-FSTQ, a Fisher-guided token quantization system primitive for communication-efficient federated LLM fine-tuning. Fed-FSTQ employs a lightweight Fisher proxy to estimate token sensitivity, coupling importance-aware token selection with non-uniform mixed-precision quantization to allocate higher fidelity to informative evidence while suppressing redundant transmission. The method is model-agnostic, serves as a drop-in module for standard federated PEFT pipelines, e.g., LoRA, without modifying the server aggregation rule, and supports bandwidth-heterogeneous clients via compact sparse message packing. Experiments on multilingual QA and medical QA under non-IID partitions show that Fed-FSTQ reduces cumulative uplink traffic required to reach a fixed quality threshold by 46x relative to a standard LoRA baseline, and improves end-to-end wall-clock time-to-accuracy by 52%. Furthermore, enabling Fisher-guided token reduction at inference yields up to a 1.55x end-to-end speedup on NVIDIA Jetson-class edge devices, demonstrating deployability under tight resource constraints.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Fed-FSTQ**, a novel system primitive designed to accelerate **federated fine-tuning** of LLMs on resource-constrained edge devices by addressing the uplink communication bottleneck.
- Implements a **lightweight Fisher proxy** to estimate token sensitivity, enabling **importance-aware token selection** and non-uniform mixed-precision quantization.
- Features a **model-agnostic** architecture that acts as a drop-in module for **PEFT pipelines** (e.g., LoRA) without requiring modifications to central server aggregation rules.
- Supports **bandwidth-heterogeneous** environments through compact sparse message packing, optimizing transmission for non-IID data distributions.
key_results: Reduces cumulative uplink traffic by 46x compared to standard LoRA baselines during training.
Improves end-to-end wall-clock time-to-accuracy by 52% in mobile deployment scenarios.
Achieves up to 1.55x end-to-end inference speedup on NVIDIA Jetson-class edge devices.
Maintains high model quality on multilingual and medical QA tasks under non-IID partitions.
Successfully suppresses redundant token transmission while preserving task-critical signals.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Fed-FSTQ, using Fisher-guided token quantization to slash federated LLM fine-tuning communication overhead by 46x.

title: Recommending Usability Improvements with Multimodal Large Language Models
summary:
Usability describes quality attributes of application user interfaces that determine how effectively users can interact with them. Traditional usability evaluation methods require considerable expertise and resources, which can be challenging, especially for small teams and organizations. Automating usability evaluation could make it more accessible and help to improve the user experience. The recent emergence of powerful multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has opened new opportunities for automating usability evaluation and recommendation of improvements. These models can process visual inputs such as images and videos alongside textual context, which enables the identification of usability issues and the generation of actionable suggestions to resolve these issues.
  In this paper, we present a novel automated approach that uses limited application context and screen recordings of user interactions as input to an MLLM. The model automatically identifies and describes usability issues based on Nielsens usability heuristics, and provides corresponding explanations and improvement recommendations. To reduce the developer effort of manual prioritization, the recommendations are ranked by severity. The quality and practical usefulness of the generated recommendations were evaluated based on a user study that involved software engineers as participants. The evaluation focused on the highest-ranked suggestions provided by the model. The results demonstrate the potential of our approach to provide low-effort usability improvement recommendations. This makes it a promising complement to traditional evaluation methods, especially in settings with limited access to usability experts. In this sense, the approach serves as a basis for future integration into development tools to enable automated usability evaluation within software engineering workflows.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel framework using **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** to automate usability audits by processing application context and screen recordings.
- Maps visual interaction data to **Nielsen’s usability heuristics** to provide structured, expert-level feedback and actionable UI recommendations.
- Implements a **severity-based ranking algorithm** for recommendations, specifically designed to reduce developer triage time and cognitive load.
- Aims to integrate **automated UX evaluation** directly into the software development lifecycle (SDLC), particularly for teams lacking dedicated usability experts.
key_results: Successfully automated the identification of usability issues from raw video interaction data.
Validated improvement recommendations through a user study with professional software engineers.
Demonstrated that MLLMs can effectively map interaction friction to established Nielsen's heuristics.
Provided a severity ranking system that aligns with developer prioritization needs.
Showcased the viability of MLLMs as low-effort substitutes for traditional, resource-intensive usability testing.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce an MLLM-based approach to automatically detect usability issues and rank improvements from user interaction recordings.

title: CoRE: A Fine-Grained Code Reasoning Benchmark Beyond Output Prediction
summary: Despite strong performance on code generation tasks, it remains unclear whether large language models (LLMs) genuinely reason about code execution. Existing code reasoning benchmarks primarily evaluate final output correctness under a single canonical implementation, leaving two critical aspects underexplored: (1) whether LLMs can maintain consistency to functionally equivalent implementations, and (2) whether LLMs can accurately reason about intermediate execution states. We introduce \textbf{CoRE}, a \textbf{Co}de \textbf{Re}asoning benchmark that evaluates code reasoning through \textbf{implementation invariance} and \textbf{process transparency}. Extensive evaluations on eight frontier LLMs reveal two fundamental limitations. First, models exhibit a substantial \textbf{robustness gap}, with performance varying significantly across equivalent implementations. Second, we observe \textbf{superficial execution}, where models arrive at correct final outputs without correctly reasoning about intermediate execution states. Together, these findings demonstrate that output-only evaluations are insufficient for assessing code reasoning and position CoRE as a necessary benchmark for evaluating robust and faithful code reasoning.\footnote{Data and code are available at https://github.com/ZJUSig/CoRE.}

processed.summary:
- Introduces **CoRE**, a diagnostic benchmark that shifts the focus of code evaluation from final output prediction to **implementation invariance** and **process transparency**.
- Evaluates LLMs on their ability to maintain consistent reasoning across **functionally equivalent** implementations, revealing a significant robustness gap.
- Analyzes **intermediate execution states** to detect "superficial execution," where models arrive at correct outputs through pattern matching rather than logical simulation.
- Provides a dataset and framework for assessing the **faithfulness** of reasoning in frontier code models.
key_results: Identified a substantial robustness gap where model performance drops significantly on equivalent code variations.
Discovered 'superficial execution' patterns: correct final outputs often stem from incorrect intermediate reasoning.
Evaluation of eight frontier LLMs shows they struggle to accurately track variable states during execution.
Proves that output-only benchmarks fail to distinguish between true reasoning and probabilistic guessing.
Establishes new metrics for implementation invariance to quantify model reliability in code tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Zhejiang University researchers introduce CoRE to expose LLM failures in code reasoning through intermediate state and implementation invariance testing.

title: Space-time excitation creates soliton trains in multimode fibers
summary: In this work, we show that injecting a single space-time-coupled light pulse-beam into a multimode graded-index fiber generates a train of multimode solitons. Space-time couplings excite the spatial modes with distinct temporal profiles. Due to nonlinear interactions, with a properly chosen input power these profiles split into several unique multimode solitons. In the case of a spatially chirped input pulse, two solitons composed of modes $LP_{01}$ and $LP_{11}$ are formed. In the case of the injection of a space-time optical vortex, characterized by its topological charge $\ell$, a train composed of $|\ell| + 1$ multimode solitons is generated. Their energy and modal composition are directly determined by the absolute value of the topological charge.

processed.summary:
• This research demonstrates that **space-time excitation** within multimode graded-index fibers can effectively generate structured **multimode soliton trains**.
• The architecture utilizes **space-time-coupled light pulses** to excite spatial modes with distinct temporal profiles, which then evolve through **nonlinear interactions**.
• By controlling the **topological charge** of the input optical vortex, the system determines the specific energy and modal composition of the resulting solitons.
key_results: Injection of space-time-coupled pulses generates a train of multimode solitons.
Spatially chirped input pulses produce specific solitons composed of the LP01 and LP11 modes.
Space-time optical vortices generate soliton trains containing a quantity of solitons equal to the topological charge plus one.
The energy of the generated solitons is directly determined by the absolute value of the topological charge.
Demonstrates a method for deterministic control over nonlinear pulse propagation in fiber systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that space-time excitation in multimode fibers enables the generation of controllable, high-energy soliton trains.

title: Benchmarking PyCaret AutoML Against IndoBERT Fine-Tuning for Sentiment Analysis on Indonesian IKN Twitter Data
summary: This paper benchmarks a classical machine learning approach based on PyCaret AutoML against a deep learning approach based on IndoBERT fine-tuning for binary sentiment analysis of Indonesian-language Twitter comments related to Ibu Kota Nusantara (IKN). The dataset contains 1,472 manually labeled samples, consisting of 780 negative and 692 positive comments. In the machine learning setting, Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, and Support Vector Machine were evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, with Logistic Regression achieving the best performance among the classical models at 77.57% accuracy and 77.17% F1-score. In the deep learning setting, the indobenchmark/indobert-base-p1 model was fine-tuned for five epochs and achieved 89.59% test accuracy and 89.37% F1-score. The results show that IndoBERT substantially outperforms the machine learning baselines, highlighting the effectiveness of Transformer-based contextual representations for informal Indonesian social media text.

processed.summary:
- Benchmarks **classical machine learning** via PyCaret against **deep learning** fine-tuning using IndoBERT for Indonesian sentiment tasks.
- Evaluates **Logistic Regression**, **Naive Bayes**, and **SVM** as baselines using a manually labeled dataset of 1,472 Twitter samples.
- Utilizes the **indobenchmark/indobert-base-p1** transformer architecture, fine-tuned over five epochs to capture localized linguistic nuances.
- Highlights the performance gap between **contextual embeddings** and traditional feature engineering for informal social media text.
key_results: IndoBERT achieved 89.59% accuracy and 89.37% F1-score on the test set.
Logistic Regression led classical models with 77.57% accuracy and 77.17% F1-score.
IndoBERT outperformed the best classical ML model by approximately 12% in accuracy.
The study utilized a specific dataset of 1,472 manually labeled samples concerning the Ibu Kota Nusantara (IKN).
Results confirm the superiority of Transformer-based models over AutoML for informal Indonesian social media syntax.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers prove that fine-tuning IndoBERT yields a 12% accuracy boost over classical AutoML for Indonesian sentiment analysis.

title: Effects on vsini determinations of O-stars from 3D model atmospheres with large turbulent velocities
summary: When studying massive stars and their life cycles, rotation plays a key role. Hence, understanding the rotation of these stars is crucial when determining their properties, or for constraining evolutionary models. We examine the reliability of the standard methods to derive projected rotation speeds vsini from photospheric spectra of hot, massive stars in the presence of large turbulent velocities. We include rotation in the spectral synthesis of O-stars by means of three-dimensional model atmospheres showing significant photospheric turbulent velocities. We then use these as mock-observations to back-test the Fourier Transform and goodness-of-fit methods commonly used for empirical determination of vsini when the turbulent velocity field is not known. When the expected vsini>v_mac, with v_mac the macroturbulent velocity, FT vsini determinations (most of the time) give reasonable results. However, if vsini < v_mac the method is no longer reliable. Results from the GOF method show that if one parameter is not significantly larger than the other, empirical best-fit values may be located in a large region of the vsini-v_mac parameter space, independent of the true values. The degenerate region follows well the empirical formula found by Howarth et al. (2007), sqrt(v_mac^2/2+vsini^2/4). In other words, only this sum can be constrained by GOF observational analysis. Our analysis shows clearly that, generally, only the sum sqrt(v_mac^2/2+vsini^2/4) can be constrained through the standard spectroscopic methods used to infer these parameters individually. Only in the case where one of the two clearly dominates, can good constraints on the dominating parameter be derived. This demonstrates that previously found empirical correlations between vsini and v_mac as well as derived statistical distributions of observed rotation rates for O-star populations will need to be re-analysed and interpreted.

processed.summary:
* The research utilizes **3D model atmospheres** with significant photospheric turbulent velocities to evaluate the accuracy of standard spectroscopic methods for determining O-star rotation.
* Analysis reveals a critical failure point in **Fourier Transform (FT) determinations** when projected rotation speed (vsini) is less than the macroturbulent velocity (v_mac).
* The study identifies a severe **parameter degeneracy** in the Goodness-of-Fit (GOF) method, where individual parameters cannot be reliably isolated from their combined effects.
* The results necessitate a **global re-evaluation** of previously established empirical correlations and statistical distributions of rotation rates for O-star populations.
key_results: FT methods for vsini determination become unreliable when vsini is less than macroturbulent velocity.
GOF methods fail to distinguish between vsini and v_mac unless one parameter significantly dominates.
Empirical best-fit values often occupy a large degenerate region in the parameter space regardless of true values.
Spectroscopic methods can generally only constrain the sum: sqrt(v_mac^2/2 + vsini^2/4).
Previously reported correlations between vsini and v_mac in O-stars are likely artifacts of analytical limitations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that O-star rotation measurements are fundamentally degenerate with turbulent velocities, requiring re-analysis of historical data.

title: GeoSearch: Augmenting Worldwide Geolocalization with Web-Scale Reverse Image Search and Image Matching
summary: Worldwide image geolocalization, which aims to predict the GPS coordinates of any image on Earth, remains challenging due to global visual diversity. Recent generative approaches based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) leverage candidates retrieved from fixed databases for reasoning, but often struggle with scenes that are absent from the reference set. In this work, we propose GeoSearch, an open-world geolocation framework that integrates web-scale reverse image search into the RAG pipeline. GeoSearch augments LMM prompts with database-retrieved coordinates and textual evidence extracted from web pages. To mitigate noise from irrelevant content, we introduce a two-layer filtering mechanism consisting of image matching, followed by confidence-based gating. Experiments on standard benchmarks Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k demonstrate the superiority of GeoSearch under leakage-aware evaluation. Our code and data are publicly available to support reproducibility.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **GeoSearch**, an open-world geolocation framework that integrates **web-scale reverse image search** into a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** pipeline.
- Employs a **two-layer filtering mechanism**—incorporating image matching and confidence-based gating—to extract high-quality textual evidence and coordinates from noisy web results.
- Enhances **Large Multimodal Model (LMM)** reasoning by augmenting prompts with a hybrid of database-retrieved GPS data and external web-scraped context.
- Addresses benchmark reliability by implementing a **leakage-aware evaluation** protocol to ensure results reflect real-world generalization.
key_results: Outperforms state-of-the-art fixed-database RAG methods on Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k benchmarks.
Effectively localizes scenes absent from standard reference sets by leveraging real-time web data.
The image matching filter successfully mitigates noise from irrelevant web content and visual outliers.
Textual evidence extraction from web pages provides critical semantic cues for LMM-based coordinate prediction.
Demonstrates superior robustness in open-world scenarios compared to closed-set retrieval systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: GeoSearch researchers develop a web-scale RAG framework using reverse image search to enhance global image geolocalization accuracy.

title: ML-SAN: Multi-Level Speaker-Adaptive Network for Emotion Recognition in Conversations
summary: To establish empathy with machines, it is essential to fully understand human emotional changes. However, research in multimodal emotion recognition often overlooks one problem: individual expressive traits vary significantly, which means that different people may express emotions differently. In our daily lives, we can see this. When communicating with different people, some express "happiness" through their facial expressions and words, while others may hide their happiness or express it through their actions. Both are expressions of 'happiness,' but such differences in emotional expression are still too difficult for machines to distinguish. Current emotion recognition remains at a 'static' level, using a single recognition model to identify all emotional styles. This "simplification" often affects the recognition results, especially in multi-turn dialogues. To address this problem, this paper introduces a novel Multi-Level Speaker Adaptive Network (ML-SAN), which, specifically, effectively addresses the challenge of speaker identity information confusion. ML-SAN does not simply assign a speaker's ID after recognition; instead, it employs a three-stage adaptive process: First, Input-level Calibration uses Feature-Level Linear Modulation (FiLM) to adjust the raw audio and visual features into a neutral space unrelated to the speaker. Then, Interaction-level Gating re-adjusts the trust level for each modality (e.g., voice or facial features) based on the speaker's identity information. Finally, Output-level Regularization maintains the consistency of speaker features in the latent space. Tests on the MELD and IEMOCAP datasets show that our model (ML-SAN) achieves better results, performs exceptionally well in handling challenging tail sentiment categories, and better addresses the diversity of speakers in real-world scenarios.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **ML-SAN**, a three-stage framework designed to decouple speaker-specific traits from emotional expressions in multimodal dialogues.
• Implements **Input-level Calibration** using **Feature-Level Linear Modulation (FiLM)** to map raw audio and visual features into a speaker-neutral space.
• Utilizes **Interaction-level Gating** to dynamically adjust the weighting of different modalities (voice and facial) based on the individual speaker's identity.
• Introduces **Output-level Regularization** to ensure consistency of speaker features in the latent space, improving performance on long-tail sentiment categories.
key_results: Achieved superior performance on the MELD and IEMOCAP benchmarks compared to static recognition models.
Successfully addressed the challenge of speaker identity confusion in multi-turn dialogues.
Significantly improved the identification of challenging tail sentiment categories.
Validated the effectiveness of FiLM for neutralizing speaker-specific expression styles.
Demonstrated that identity-aware gating enhances the reliability of audio and visual feature fusion.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ML-SAN to improve multimodal emotion recognition by normalizing speaker-specific expressive traits through adaptive gating.

title: CoRE: Concept-Reasoning Expansion for Continual Brain Lesion Segmentation
summary: Accurate brain lesion segmentation in MRI is vital for effective clinical diagnosis and treatment planning. Due to high annotation costs and strict data privacy regulations, universal models require employing Continual Learning (CL) to adapt to evolving clinical tasks without losing previously acquired knowledge. However, existing CL paradigms often suffer from capacity limits or redundant parameter growth, and even advanced dynamic methods rely mostly on image-perception strategies that struggle to handle the substantial pathological and multimodal heterogeneity inherent in brain imaging. To address this issue, we propose Concept-Reasoning Expansion (CoRE) framework, which establishes a joint decision-making mechanism by integrating visual features with structured concepts. Through the alignment of image tokens with a hierarchical concept library, CoRE simulates clinical reasoning to guide both interpretable expert routing and demand-based model growth. This collaborative process ensures model evolution is grounded in clinical priors, preventing redundant parameter expansion while maximizing knowledge reuse. Extensive evaluations across 12 sequential brain lesion MRI tasks demonstrate that CoRE achieves state-of-the-art performance and provides a high knowledge starting point for efficient future adaptation. Its superior few-shot transferability and clinical interpretability further validate its effectiveness in managing non-stationary clinical data streams. Our code will be released soon.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **CoRE (Concept-Reasoning Expansion)**, a framework designed for **Continual Learning (CL)** in brain lesion segmentation to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
- Integrates **visual features with a hierarchical concept library**, aligning image tokens with clinical priors to simulate human-like **clinical reasoning**.
- Implements **demand-based model growth** and **interpretable expert routing**, ensuring efficient parameter expansion while maximizing cross-task knowledge reuse.
- Demonstrates high performance across 12 sequential MRI tasks with enhanced **few-shot transferability** and model interpretability.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across 12 sequential brain lesion MRI tasks.
Mitigated catastrophic forgetting without the redundant parameter growth common in dynamic methods.
Improved model interpretability by grounding decision-making in clinical concepts.
Demonstrated superior few-shot transferability for rapid adaptation to new clinical environments.
Established a robust knowledge baseline that facilitates efficient learning of future tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CoRE, a concept-reasoning framework that improves continual brain lesion segmentation through hierarchical clinical priors.

title: Multi-action Tangled Program Graphs for Multi-task Reinforcement Learning with Continuous Control
summary: Over the past few decades, machine learning has been widely used to learn complex tasks. Reinforcement Learning (RL), inspired by human behavior, is a great example, as it involves developing specific behaviours for specific tasks. To further challenge algorithms, Multi-Task RL (MTRL) environments have been introduced, requiring a single model to learn multiple behaviors. The Tangled Program Graph (TPG) algorithm is a Genetic Programming (GP) algorithm designed for discrete MTRL environments. Recently, the MAPLE algorithm has been proposed, as another GP algorithm that achieves high results in single task continuous RL environments. A variation of the TPG is proposed alongside MAPLE, named Multi-Action TPG (MATPG) that aggregates MAPLE agents, and creates a control flow to activate them. Initially tested on single task RL environments only, MATPG achieved similar results to MAPLE. In this work, we present a new benchmark based on the MuJoCo Half Cheetah from Gymnasium. This benchmark features five distinct obstacles that are randomly positioned in front of the agent, each of which demands a unique behavior. This benchmark serves as a use case for MATPG, to prove its ability as a GP solution for continuous MTRL environments. Our experiments demonstrate its superiority in this multi-task use case when combined with lexicase selection. Furthermore, we examine the interpretability of the evolved graph, revealing that the decision flow of the model is fully interpretable.

• Introduces **Multi-Action Tangled Program Graphs (MATPG)**, a Genetic Programming (GP) framework designed for **continuous control Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning (MTRL)**.
• Architecture leverages **MAPLE agents** as action nodes within a control flow graph, allowing the model to dynamically activate specific sub-policies based on environmental context.
• Employs **Lexicase selection** to evolve diverse behaviors, enabling a single model to navigate complex environments with distinct obstacles.
• Provides high **interpretability**, as the decision logic within the evolved graph structure can be directly visualized and analyzed compared to black-box neural networks.

Demonstrates superior performance on a custom MuJoCo Half Cheetah benchmark featuring five distinct, randomly positioned obstacles.
Proves that the integration of lexicase selection significantly improves multi-task performance over standard evolutionary selection methods.
Evolved models maintain performance parity with specialized single-task algorithms while handling multiple behaviors within a single model.
The evolved control flow graphs provide a fully interpretable execution path for debugging and strategy analysis.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MATPG, a Genetic Programming framework achieving superior multi-task continuous control with fully interpretable decision graphs.

title: Plausible but Wrong: A case study on Agentic Failures in Astrophysical Workflows
summary: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being integrated into scientific workflows, yet their behavior under realistic conditions remains insufficiently understood. We evaluate CMBAgent across two workflow paradigms and eighteen astrophysical tasks. In the One-Shot setting, access to domain-specific context yields an approximately ~6x performance improvement (0.85 vs. ~0 without context), with the primary failure mode being silent incorrect computation - syntactically valid code that produces plausible but inaccurate results. In the Deep Research setting, the system frequently exhibits silent failures across stress tests, producing physically inconsistent posteriors without self-diagnosis. Overall, performance is strong on well-specified tasks but degrades on problems designed to probe reasoning limits, often without visible error signals. These findings highlight that the most concerning failure mode in agentic scientific workflows is not overt failure, but confident generation of incorrect results. We release our evaluation framework to facilitate systematic reliability analysis of scientific AI agents.

processed.summary:
• Evaluates **CMBAgent**, an autonomous system designed for astrophysical workflows, across eighteen distinct tasks using **One-Shot** and **Deep Research** paradigms.
• Implements **domain-specific context** retrieval to achieve a **6x performance improvement**, increasing task success from near-zero to 0.85.
• Identifies **silent incorrect computation** as the primary risk, where agents produce syntactically valid code that generates **physically inconsistent** or plausible-looking but wrong data.
• Proposes a specialized **evaluation framework** to stress-test the reasoning limits and self-diagnosis capabilities of scientific agents.
key_results: Domain-specific context increased one-shot performance from ~0 to 0.85.
The most common failure mode is 'silent failure'—incorrect results without syntax errors.
Deep Research agents failed to self-diagnose physically inconsistent posterior distributions.
System performance significantly degrades on tasks designed to probe complex reasoning limits.
Released a new framework for systematic reliability analysis of scientific AI agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that scientific agents frequently generate plausible but physically incorrect results without providing any error signals.

title: Proximity Ferroelectricity Driven by Mobile High-Miller-Index Domain Walls
summary: Wurtzite ferroelectrics such as scandium-doped aluminum nitride (AlScN) are promising for next-generation memory because of their compatibility with semiconductor processes and strong spontaneous polarization. Ferroelectric switching in these materials is typically attributed to doping-induced softening of the bulk switching barrier. However, recent reports of proximity ferroelectricity, in which undoped AlN layers up to 500 nm thick fully switch in AlN/AlScN multilayers, challenge this view. Here, we reveal an alternative switching mechanism mediated by high-Miller-index domain walls, long overlooked due to their complex geometry and presumed instability. Using first-principles calculations and machine-learning molecular dynamics simulations, we show that these walls, once nucleated, migrate with exceptionally low barriers. The Sc dopants play a dual role: they stabilize high-index walls and thereby promote nucleation, while also introducing pinning that hinders wall motion. In multilayers, our simulations demonstrate that mobile domain walls nucleated in AlScN can propagate deep into adjacent AlN, where they move easily without dopant pinning, enabling low-field switching across thick undoped layers. This microscopic divide-and-conquer mechanism resolves the puzzle of proximity ferroelectricity and highlights high-index interfaces as an underexplored lever for controlling ferroelectric switching.

processed.summary:
- Employs **machine-learning molecular dynamics (MLMD)** and first-principles calculations to investigate ferroelectric switching in Sc-doped aluminum nitride.
- Identifies **high-Miller-index domain walls** as highly mobile interfaces that migrate with exceptionally low energy barriers once nucleated.
- Proposes a **divide-and-conquer mechanism** where Sc dopants facilitate nucleation while undoped AlN layers allow unimpeded wall propagation.
key_results: Discovery that high-index domain walls are the primary drivers of proximity ferroelectricity.
MLMD simulations reveal Sc dopants stabilize wall nucleation but introduce motion-hindering pinning.
Undoped AlN layers up to 500 nm thick can fully switch when adjacent to doped AlScN.
Low-field switching is achieved by separating the nucleation and propagation phases across material layers.
The research highlights high-index interfaces as a novel lever for ferroelectric memory control.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize machine-learning molecular dynamics to uncover low-barrier ferroelectric switching mechanisms in semiconductor-compatible multilayers.

title: VAE-Inf: A statistically interpretable generative paradigm for imbalanced classification
summary: Imbalanced classification remains a pervasive challenge in machine learning, particularly when minority samples are too scarce to provide a robust discriminative boundary. In such extreme scenarios, conventional models often suffer from unstable decision boundaries and a lack of reliable error control. To bridge the gap between generative modeling and discriminative classification, we propose a two-stage framework \textbf{VAE-Inf} that integrates deep representation learning with statistically interpretable hypothesis testing. In the first stage, we adopt a one-class modeling perspective by training a variational autoencoder (VAE) exclusively on majority-class data to capture the underlying reference distribution. The resulting latent posteriors are aggregated via a Wasserstein barycenter to construct a global Gaussian reference model, providing a geometrically principled baseline for the majority class. In the second stage, we transform this generative foundation into a discriminative classifier by fine-tuning the encoder with limited minority samples. This is achieved through a novel distribution-aware loss that enforces probabilistic separation between classes based on variance-normalized projection statistics. For inference, we introduce a projection-based score that admits a natural hypothesis testing interpretation, allowing for a distribution-free calibration procedure. This approach yields exact finite-sample control of the Type-I error (false positive rate) without relying on restrictive parametric assumptions. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves competitive performance against other approaches. The codes are available upon request.

* Proposes **VAE-Inf**, a two-stage framework that bridges generative modeling and discriminative classification for extreme **imbalanced data** scenarios.
* Utilizes **Variational Autoencoders (VAE)** to model the majority class, aggregating latent posteriors via **Wasserstein barycenter** to create a robust Gaussian reference.
* Introduces a **distribution-aware loss** for fine-tuning the encoder, ensuring probabilistic separation between classes based on variance-normalized statistics.
* Provides a **statistically interpretable inference** mechanism that allows for exact finite-sample control of the **Type-I error** without parametric assumptions.
key_results: Successfully integrated deep representation learning with hypothesis testing for classification.
Established a geometrically principled baseline for majority-class distribution using latent aggregation.
Developed a novel projection-based score for distribution-free calibration during inference.
Achieved finite-sample control over false positive rates in imbalanced scenarios.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce VAE-Inf, combining generative VAEs with hypothesis testing to reliably classify extremely imbalanced datasets.

title: AHASD: Asynchronous Heterogeneous Architecture for LLM Adaptive Drafting Speculative Decoding on Mobile Devices
summary: Speculative decoding enhances the inference efficiency of large language models (LLMs) by generating drafts using a small draft language model (DLM) and verifying them in batches with a large target language model (TLM). However, adaptive drafting inference on a mobile single-NPU-PIM system faces idle overhead in traditional operator-level synchronous execution and wasted computation in asynchronous execution due to fluctuations in draft length. This paper introduces AHASD, a task-level asynchronous mobile NPU-PIM heterogeneous architecture for speculative decoding. Notably, AHASD achieves parallel drafting on the PIM and verification on a single NPU through task-level DLM-TLM decoupling and specifically, it incorporates Entropy-History-Aware Drafting Control and Time-Aware Pre-Verification Control to dynamically manage adaptive drafting algorithm execution and pre-verification timing, suppressing invalid drafting based on low-confidence drafts. Additionally, AHASD integrates Attention Algorithm Units and Gated Task Scheduling Units within LPDDR5-PIM to enable attention link localization and sub-microsecond task switching on the PIM side. Experimental results for different LLMs and adaptive drafting algorithms show that AHASD achieves up to 4.2$\times$ in throughput and 5.6$\times$ in energy efficiency improvements over a GPU-only baseline, and 1.5$\times$ in throughput and 1.24$\times$ in energy efficiency gains over the state-of-the-art GPU+PIM baseline, with hardware overhead below 3\% of the DRAM area.

processed.summary:
- **AHASD** implements a task-level asynchronous architecture that decouples **Draft Language Model (DLM)** drafting on PIM from **Target Language Model (TLM)** verification on NPU.
- It features **Entropy-History-Aware Drafting Control** to dynamically suppress invalid drafts based on token confidence, significantly reducing wasted compute cycles on mobile hardware.
- The architecture integrates **Attention Algorithm Units** and **Gated Task Scheduling Units** directly into LPDDR5-PIM to enable sub-microsecond task switching.
- Specifically optimized for **Local LLMs**, it resolves the idle overhead issues inherent in traditional synchronous operator-level execution on heterogeneous mobile systems.
key_results: Achieves up to 4.2x throughput improvement compared to mobile GPU-only baselines.
Delivers 5.6x higher energy efficiency over standard mobile GPU configurations.
Outperforms state-of-the-art GPU+PIM hybrid systems by 1.5x in throughput.
Hardware area overhead is minimized to less than 3% of the LPDDR5 DRAM area.
Successfully manages adaptive drafting lengths to mitigate fluctuations in inference latency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce AHASD, a mobile architecture using asynchronous NPU-PIM decoupling to accelerate LLM speculative decoding throughput by 4.2x.

title: R$^3$-SQL: Ranking Reward and Resampling for Text-to-SQL
summary: Modern Text-to-SQL systems generate multiple candidate SQL queries and rank them to judge a final prediction. However, existing methods face two limitations. First, they often score functionally equivalent SQL queries inconsistently despite identical execution results. Second, ranking cannot recover when the correct SQL is absent from the candidate pool. We propose R$^3$-SQL, a Text-to-SQL framework that addresses both issues through unified reward for ranking and resampling. R$^3$-SQL first groups candidates by execution result and ranks groups for consistency. To score each group, it combines a pairwise preference across groups with a pointwise utility from the best group rank and size, capturing relative preference, consistency, and candidate quality. To improve candidate recall, R$^3$-SQL introduces agentic resampling, which judges the generated candidate pool and selectively resamples when the correct SQL is likely absent. R$^3$-SQL achieves 75.03 execution accuracy on BIRD-dev, a new state of the art among methods using models with disclosed sizes, with consistent gains across five benchmarks.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **R3-SQL**, a framework that addresses Text-to-SQL ranking inconsistencies by grouping candidate queries based on their **execution results**.
- Implements a **unified reward system** that combines pairwise preference between groups with pointwise utility, effectively measuring relative quality and candidate consistency.
- Features an **agentic resampling** mechanism that evaluates the generated candidate pool and triggers new generation cycles when the correct SQL is likely missing.
- Optimizes the **ranking-to-resampling** pipeline to achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex benchmarks like BIRD-dev.
key_results: Achieved 75.03% execution accuracy on the BIRD-dev benchmark.
Established a new SOTA for models with disclosed sizes in Text-to-SQL tasks.
Demonstrated consistent performance gains across five major SQL benchmarks.
Reduced scoring variance by evaluating functionally equivalent SQL groups rather than strings.
Improved candidate recall through the iterative agentic resampling approach.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce R3-SQL, using execution-based ranking and agentic resampling to achieve state-of-the-art Text-to-SQL accuracy.

title: ANCHOR: A Physically Grounded Closed-Loop Framework for Robust Home-Service Mobile Manipulation
summary: Recent advances in open-vocabulary mobile manipulation have brought robots into real domestic environments. In such settings, reliable long-horizon execution under open-set object references and frequent disturbances becomes essential. However, many failures persist. These are not caused by semantic misunderstanding but by inconsistencies between symbolic plans and the evolving physical world, manifested as three recurring limitations: (i) existing systems often rely on pre-scanned semantic maps that become inconsistent after scene changes and disturbances; (ii) they select navigation endpoints without considering downstream manipulation feasibility, causing the "arrived but inoperable" problem; and (iii) they handle anomalies through undifferentiated global replanning, which often fails to contain local errors. To address this execution inconsistency, we present ANCHOR, a physically grounded closed-loop framework that aligns symbolic reasoning with verifiable physical state during execution. ANCHOR integrates three mechanisms: (i) physically anchored task planning, which binds symbolic predicates to observable geometric anchors and re-validates them after each action; (ii) operability-aware base alignment, which ensures that navigation endpoints satisfy kinematic reachability and local collision feasibility; and (iii) minimum-responsible-layer hierarchical recovery, which localizes failures across perception, base-arm coordination, and execution layers to prevent cascading retries. Across 60 real-robot trials in previously unseen environments, ANCHOR improves task success from 53.3% to 71.7% and achieves a 71.4% recovery rate under perturbations, demonstrating that explicit physical grounding and structured failure containment are critical for robust mobile manipulation. Our project page is available at https://anchor9178.github.io/ANCHOR/ .

processed.summary:
- **ANCHOR** is a closed-loop mobile manipulation framework that synchronizes **symbolic reasoning** with real-time physical constraints to eliminate the "arrived but inoperable" failure mode.
- The architecture features **physically anchored task planning**, which binds symbolic predicates to observable geometric data rather than relying on static, error-prone semantic maps.
- The system integrates an **operability-aware base alignment** module and a **hierarchical recovery** mechanism to ensure kinematic reachability and localized error handling across perception and execution layers.
key_results: Improved task success rate from 53.3% to 71.7% in real-robot domestic environments.
Achieved a 71.4% recovery rate under physical disturbances and scene changes.
Successfully validated the framework across 60 real-world trials in previously unseen locations.
Demonstrated significant reduction in cascading failures through localized hierarchical replanning.
Verified that aligning navigation endpoints with kinematic reachability prevents downstream manipulation failures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The ANCHOR Research Team introduces a physically grounded framework that improves robot task success by aligning symbolic reasoning with geometric constraints.

title: Assessment of the quantitative impact of occlusal positioning splints on temporomandibular joint conditions
summary: A computational method for quantitative analysis of temporomandibular joint (TMJ) configuration using occlusal positioning splints is proposed and demonstrated. The method models a positioning splint as a physical realization of a predefined rigid transformation of the mandible, derived from multimodal data, including CBCT, facial motion acquisition, and dental scans integrated within a common coordinate system. Splints corresponding to selected mandibular positions are designed and fabricated, and their positioning accuracy is evaluated using repeated scans of plaster models. Discrepancies are represented as error transformations and analyzed statistically in the space of rigid motions. The estimated transformations are propagated to segmented TMJ structures, enabling simulation-based evaluation of joint space changes. Transformation-based error analysis and surface distance metrics are used to quantify differences between planned and achieved configurations. The method enables indirect assessment of TMJ configuration using a single anatomical model and transformation data, reducing the need for repeated imaging across multiple mandibular positions. This study is intended as a methodological demonstration, supported by a clear step-by-step graphical presentation, and does not aim to provide clinical validation.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **computational framework** for modeling temporomandibular joint (TMJ) configurations by treating occlusal splints as **physical rigid transformations**.
• Integrates **multimodal data** (CBCT, facial motion, and dental scans) into a unified coordinate system to simulate joint space changes.
• Employs **transformation-based error analysis** and surface distance metrics to quantify the discrepancy between planned and actual mandibular positions.
• Enables indirect assessment of joint conditions, significantly reducing the necessity for **repeated anatomical imaging** and radiation exposure.
key_results: Established a method for modeling positioning splints as predefined rigid transformations of the mandible.
Integrated CBCT, facial motion acquisition, and dental scans into a common coordinate system.
Validated positioning accuracy through statistical analysis of rigid motion errors in plaster models.
Enabled simulation-based evaluation of TMJ structural changes using a single base anatomical model.
Demonstrated a quantitative workflow for TMJ analysis that avoids repeated medical imaging sequences.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a computational method using rigid transformations to simulate TMJ changes, reducing the need for repeated imaging.

title: FusionCIM: Accelerating LLM Inference with Fusion-Driven Computing-in-Memory Architecture
summary: In this paper, we propose FusionCIM, an operator-fusion-driven compute-in-memory (CIM) accelerator architecture for efficient and scalable LLM inference, with three key innovations: (1) a hybrid CIM pipeline architecture that maps QKT computation on inner-product-based CIM (IP-CIM) and PV aggregation on outer-product-based CIM (OP-CIM) for efficient matrix multiplications fusion; (2) a QO-stationary dataflow that eliminates repeated KV loading in CIM and K-matrix access in buffer under transpose fusion, significantly improving data reuse on chip; and (3) a pattern-aware online-softmax mechanism that exploits distribution regularities of attention scores to reduce exponential rescaling overhead for non-linear fusion. Experimental results on LLaMA-3 model show that FusionCIM achieves up to 3.86x energy saving, and 1.98x speedup compared with prior SOTA CIM-based designs with 29.4 TOPS/W energy efficiency at the system level.

processed.summary:

- Utilizes a **hybrid CIM pipeline** that combines inner-product (IP-CIM) for QKT and outer-product (OP-CIM) for PV aggregation to maximize matrix multiplication efficiency.
- Implements a **QO-stationary dataflow** and **pattern-aware online-softmax** to minimize data movement and redundant KV loading, which are major bottlenecks in standard architectures.
- Provides a scalable hardware solution for **Local LLMs**, significantly enhancing throughput while maintaining a high energy efficiency profile.
key_results: 3.86x energy saving compared to existing SOTA CIM-based designs.
1.98x speedup for LLaMA-3 model inference.
Achieved system-level energy efficiency of 29.4 TOPS/W.
Eliminated repeated KV loading and K-matrix access during transpose fusion.
Reduced exponential rescaling overhead through distribution-regularity-aware softmax fusion.
